text: Rudolf Bultmann’s demythologization program had never been favourably received within the Evangelical world of Afrikaans South Africa, and by extension had not been taken seriously. Almost 70 years have now passed since his 1941 treatise on demythologization, sparked a tumultuous debate world-wide, and Bultmann’s name is barely mentioned in contemporary scholarship. This article now offers a timely appraisal in light of Bultmann’s 130th birthday. By engaging afresh with the words of Bultmann, irrespective of the scholarship of the intervening decades, it hopes to make a contribution to a re-exploration of his demythologization programme in a world far removed from the theological debate of mid-century. The intervening years have given sufficient time for the issues of that debate to settle, and one of the purposes of this article is to enquire into whether Bultmann’s contributions to the discussion are in any way enduring, and therefore relevant for contemporary reflection.
explanation: Polished academic prose like this can be produced by AI, so the text could be AI-generated. However, the slightly archaic phrasing and nuanced scholarly focus add some evidence of human authorship, yielding moderate uncertainty.
